prompt: What must you do at a steady red traffic light?
choices: Slow down and proceed if clear
Stop only if other cars are present
Yield to traffic on your left
explanation: At a red light you must come to a complete stop at the stop line, or before the crosswalk or intersection if there is no stop line.

prompt: A steady yellow traffic light means:
choices: Speed up to clear the intersection
The light is about to turn red; stop if you can do so safely
explanation: A yellow light warns that the signal is about to change to red. Stop if you can do so safely; do not speed up to beat the light.

prompt: When may you turn right on a red light in Virginia?
choices: After coming to a complete stop and yielding, unless a sign prohibits it
Only if no sign is posted, without stopping
explanation: You may turn right on red after a complete stop and yielding to traffic and pedestrians, unless a 'No Turn on Red' sign is posted.

prompt: A flashing red traffic signal means:
choices: Stop, then proceed when safe, as at a stop sign
The signal is broken; ignore it
explanation: A flashing red light means the same as a stop sign: come to a complete stop, then proceed when it is safe to do so.

prompt: A flashing yellow traffic signal means:
choices: Slow down and proceed with caution
explanation: A flashing yellow light means slow down and proceed with caution.

prompt: When traffic signals are completely dark because of a power outage, you should:
choices: Treat the intersection as an all-way stop
Wait for the lights to come back on
explanation: When signals are not displaying any lights, you must stop and proceed as though the intersection were an all-way stop.

prompt: A green arrow signal means:
choices: You may go in the direction of the arrow if the way is clear
Yield to all traffic before turning
Pedestrians have the right of way only
explanation: A green arrow gives a protected movement: you may proceed in the direction of the arrow if the way is clear.

prompt: You want to turn left at a green light with no green arrow. You must:
choices: Turn immediately, you have the right of way
Yield to oncoming traffic and pedestrians, then turn when clear
Stop and wait for a green arrow
explanation: On a solid green light, left-turning traffic must yield to oncoming vehicles and pedestrians before completing the turn.

prompt: An eight-sided (octagon) sign always means:
explanation: The octagon shape is reserved exclusively for stop signs and always means come to a complete stop.

prompt: A red circle with a slash through a symbol means:
choices: NO — the action shown is prohibited
explanation: A red circle with a slash means NO: the symbol shows an action that is not allowed.

prompt: A yellow, diamond-shaped sign is used to:
choices: Give a regulation you must obey
Warn of conditions or hazards ahead
explanation: Diamond-shaped warning signs alert you to special conditions or hazards ahead — slow down and be ready to react.

prompt: Broken yellow center lines on a two-lane road mean:
choices: Passing on the left is allowed in either direction when clear
explanation: Yellow lines separate opposing traffic. A broken yellow center line means passing on the left is allowed in either direction when the way ahead is clear.

prompt: A solid yellow line next to a broken yellow line means:
choices: Passing is allowed only from the broken-line side
explanation: Passing is allowed from the side of the broken line, but not from the side of the solid yellow line.

prompt: White lines on the road separate:
choices: Lanes of traffic moving in the same direction
explanation: Broken white lines separate lanes of traffic traveling in the same direction; you may change lanes with caution.

prompt: A five-sided (pentagon) sign indicates:
choices: A school zone or school crossing
explanation: The pentagon shape marks school zones and warns of school crossings.

prompt: A round (circular) sign is most often used for:
explanation: The round shape is used for railroad advance-warning signs.

prompt: At a four-way stop where two vehicles arrive at the same time, who goes first?
explanation: When two vehicles reach an intersection at the same time, the driver on the left must yield to the driver on the right.

prompt: When approaching a roundabout, you must:
choices: Yield to traffic already in the roundabout and enter when there is a gap
Come to a stop in the circle to let others in
explanation: Traffic in a roundabout has the right of way. Yield to vehicles already circulating and enter when there is a safe gap.

prompt: You must yield the right-of-way to:
choices: Pedestrians in a crosswalk and emergency vehicles using lights/sirens
No one if you have a green light
explanation: You must yield to pedestrians in crosswalks and to emergency vehicles displaying flashing lights and/or sirens.

prompt: When you approach an emergency vehicle stopped on the roadside with lights flashing, Virginia law says you should:
choices: Move over to another lane, or slow down if you cannot move over
explanation: Move over to a non-adjacent lane if you can. If you cannot, slow down to a safe speed (10 mph below the posted limit) and pass with caution.

prompt: A driver entering a road from a driveway or private road must:
choices: Yield to vehicles already on the road
Stop all traffic on the road
explanation: Vehicles entering or crossing a roadway from a driveway or private road must yield to traffic already on the road.

prompt: At an intersection with a yield sign, you should:
choices: Always come to a full stop
Slow down, be ready to stop, and give the right-of-way to traffic and pedestrians
explanation: A yield sign means slow down, be prepared to stop, and let cross traffic and pedestrians go before you proceed.

prompt: Unless otherwise posted, the speed limit in a Virginia school, business, or residential area is:
explanation: Unless a sign states otherwise, the maximum speed for passenger vehicles in school, business and residential areas is 25 MPH.

prompt: Unless otherwise posted, the maximum speed limit on most other (non-residential, paved) Virginia roads is:
explanation: Unless otherwise posted, the maximum speed limit is 55 MPH for all roads other than residential/business/school areas (25) and unpaved roads (35).

prompt: You can be charged with reckless driving in Virginia if you drive:
choices: 20 or more MPH above the limit, or over 85 MPH
Only if you cause a crash
explanation: Driving 20 or more MPH above the speed limit, or faster than 85 MPH, can bring a reckless driving charge regardless of the posted limit.

prompt: A 'speed limit' is best described as:
choices: The slowest you may legally drive
The maximum legal speed under ideal conditions
The speed you must drive at all times
explanation: A speed limit is the maximum legal speed under ideal conditions. You may drive slower, and you must drive slower when conditions are poor.

prompt: The safe following distance for speeds under 35 MPH is at least:
explanation: Use a 2-second following distance under 35 MPH, 3 seconds at 35-45 MPH, and 4 seconds at 46-70 MPH.

prompt: On wet or slippery roads, you should increase your following distance because:
explanation: Wet, icy or slippery surfaces increase stopping distance, so you need a larger space cushion to stop safely.

prompt: The three factors that determine total stopping distance are:
choices: Perception time, reaction distance, and braking distance
explanation: Total stopping distance is the sum of perception time, reaction distance, and braking distance.

prompt: Using a radar detector in Virginia is:
explanation: It is illegal to use radar detectors in Virginia.

prompt: Before changing lanes, you should:
choices: Signal, check mirrors, and check your blind spot over your shoulder
explanation: Signal your intention, check your mirrors, and glance over your shoulder to check the blind spot before changing lanes.

prompt: When passing another vehicle, it is legal to:
choices: Exceed the speed limit to pass quickly
Complete the pass before a no-passing zone and without exceeding the speed limit
Pass on a hill or curve
explanation: You must not exceed the speed limit while passing, and you must complete the pass before reaching a no-passing zone.

prompt: In Virginia, a U-turn is treated as:
choices: Two left turns, and is illegal where a 'No Left Turn' sign is posted
explanation: U-turns are considered two left turns and are illegal where a No Left Turn sign is posted.

prompt: To make a right turn, you should generally be in:
choices: The lane furthest to the right
explanation: Position your vehicle in the lane furthest to the right before making a right turn.

prompt: When should you signal before turning or changing lanes?
choices: Well in advance, to warn other drivers
Only if other cars are near
explanation: Signal in advance so other drivers and pedestrians know your intentions before you begin the maneuver.

prompt: If your vehicle begins to skid, you should:
choices: Steer in the direction you want the vehicle to go and ease off the gas
Turn the wheel sharply the opposite way
explanation: Ease off the accelerator and steer in the direction you want the front of the vehicle to go; avoid braking hard, which can worsen a skid.

prompt: When you must stop for a stopped school bus with flashing red lights and an extended stop arm, you must remain stopped until:
choices: All children are clear of the road and the bus moves again
explanation: Stay stopped until all children are clear of the roadway and the bus begins to move again.

prompt: On an undivided road, when a school bus stops with red lights flashing, which vehicles must stop?
choices: Only vehicles in the same lane
explanation: On an undivided roadway, vehicles approaching from any direction must stop for a stopped school bus with flashing red lights.

prompt: When following a motorcycle, you should:
choices: Follow closely so it can see you
explanation: Allow extra following distance behind a motorcycle, since motorcycles can stop quickly and are harder to see.

prompt: Before turning, changing lanes, or opening your door, you should always check for:
choices: Nothing if you have a green light
explanation: Always check for bicyclists and motorcyclists — they are smaller and easy to miss — before pulling out, turning, changing lanes, or opening a door.

prompt: Large trucks have big blind spots, so you should:
choices: Avoid lingering beside or directly behind them
explanation: Trucks have large blind spots (No-Zones). Avoid lingering beside or right behind a truck; if you can't see the driver's mirrors, they can't see you.

prompt: When you see a pedestrian with a white cane or guide dog at a crossing, you must:
choices: Yield and let them cross safely
explanation: Pedestrians using a white cane or guide dog have the right of way; yield and allow them to cross safely.

prompt: You must use your headlights:
choices: From sunset to sunrise and whenever you use your windshield wipers in bad weather
Only when other cars are present
explanation: Use headlights from sunset to sunrise and any time conditions (rain, snow, fog) require your wipers or reduce visibility.

prompt: In heavy fog, you should use:
explanation: Use low-beam headlights in fog. High beams reflect off the fog and glare back, reducing your visibility.

prompt: You should dim your high beams to low when an oncoming vehicle is within:
choices: You never need to dim them
explanation: Dim your high beams to low beams when within 500 feet of an oncoming vehicle, and when following another vehicle closely, to avoid blinding other drivers.

prompt: To reduce glare from the headlights of an oncoming vehicle at night, you should look:
choices: Toward the right edge of your lane
explanation: Look toward the right edge of the road or your lane to avoid being blinded by oncoming headlights.

prompt: Properly adjusted side mirrors should let you:
choices: Barely see the sides of your own vehicle to reduce blind spots
explanation: Adjust side mirrors so you can just barely see the sides of your vehicle; this widens your view and reduces blind spots.

prompt: If your vehicle starts to hydroplane on a wet road, you should:
choices: Ease off the gas and avoid sudden steering or braking
explanation: If you hydroplane, ease off the accelerator and keep the steering steady; sudden braking or steering can cause a skid.

prompt: When driving in rain, snow, or ice, you should:
choices: Drive at the posted limit regardless
Reduce your speed and increase following distance
explanation: In poor conditions, slow down below the posted limit if needed and increase your following distance to allow for longer stopping.

prompt: If you see a deer crossing sign and it is dusk, you should:
choices: Speed up to pass the area quickly
Slow down and stay alert, especially at dawn and dusk
Ignore it unless you see a deer
explanation: Deer are most active at dawn and dusk. Slow down, stay alert, and be ready to stop where deer crossing signs are posted.

prompt: If a deer or large animal is suddenly in your path and you cannot stop, you should:
choices: Brake firmly and stay in your lane rather than swerving
explanation: Brake firmly and try to stay in your lane. Swerving can cause a more serious crash with another vehicle or a fixed object.

prompt: If your vehicle's wheels drift off onto the road shoulder, you should:
choices: Brake hard and jerk the wheel back
Ease off the gas, slow down, then steer back gently when it's safe
explanation: Don't overcorrect. Ease off the accelerator, slow down, and steer gently back onto the roadway when it is safe.

prompt: When driving in heavy rain, your tires can lose contact with the road. This is called:
explanation: Hydroplaning happens when a layer of water gets between your tires and the road, causing a loss of traction.

prompt: For a driver age 21 or older, the blood alcohol concentration (BAC) at which they are considered to be driving under the influence is:
explanation: Drivers 21 and older are considered to be driving under the influence (DUI) at a BAC of .08 percent or higher.

prompt: Under Virginia's zero-tolerance law, a driver under age 21 can be charged with illegal alcohol consumption at a BAC of:
choices: At least .02 but less than .08 percent
explanation: If you are under 21, a BAC of at least .02 but less than .08 can result in a conviction for illegal consumption of alcohol.

prompt: Drinking alcohol affects your driving by:
explanation: Alcohol impairs your coordination, vision, and judgment — all critical to safe driving — even at low levels.

prompt: The only thing that will reliably sober up a person who has been drinking is:
explanation: Only time allows your body to eliminate alcohol. Coffee, showers, and fresh air do not lower your BAC.

prompt: Texting or using a handheld phone while driving is dangerous because it causes:
choices: Visual, manual, and mental distraction at once
explanation: Texting takes your eyes off the road, your hands off the wheel, and your mind off driving — visual, manual, and cognitive distraction together.

prompt: If you become drowsy while driving, the safest action is to:
choices: Open the window and keep going
Stop and rest, or change drivers
explanation: The only safe cure for drowsiness is to stop driving and rest or let someone else drive. Drowsy driving slows reactions like alcohol does.

prompt: Aggressive driving behaviors such as tailgating and weaving through traffic:
choices: Save significant time and are legal
Increase the risk of a crash and can bring penalties
Are fine if you are in a hurry
Are only a problem at night
explanation: Aggressive driving greatly raises crash risk and can result in reckless or aggressive driving charges and license suspension.

prompt: If you refuse a breath test or have a BAC of .08 or higher when charged with DUI, your license is automatically suspended for a first offense for:
explanation: An administrative license suspension of 7 days applies to a first DUI offense when you refuse the breath test or register .08 or higher.

prompt: If you are involved in a crash, you must:
choices: Leave if no police are present
Stop, help the injured, and exchange information
Move the cars and forget it
explanation: You must stop at the scene, render aid to anyone injured, and exchange information. Leaving the scene is a serious offense.

prompt: When a law enforcement officer signals you to pull over, you should:
choices: Stop in the travel lane immediately
Pull over to the right safely, stop, and keep your hands in view
Speed up to find a police station
Get out and walk to the officer
explanation: Pull over safely to the right, stop, stay in the vehicle, and keep your hands in plain view, preferably on the wheel.

prompt: During a traffic stop, you should keep your seat belt fastened and:
choices: Keep your hands in plain view and follow the officer's instructions
Reach into the glove box right away
explanation: Stay buckled, keep your hands visible, and follow the officer's directions. Reach for documents only when asked.

prompt: If your vehicle stalls on railroad tracks and a train is coming, you should:
choices: Stay in the car and try to restart it
Get everyone out and away from the tracks immediately
Wait for help in the car
explanation: Get yourself and all passengers out and far away from the tracks at once; do not try to restart or move the vehicle.

prompt: If you approach a railroad crossing with a gate that is down and lights flashing, you must:
choices: Drive around the gate if no train is visible
Stop and wait until the gate rises and lights stop
explanation: Never drive around a lowered gate. Stop and wait until the gates are fully raised and the lights stop flashing.

prompt: Under Virginia law, who must wear a safety belt?
choices: The driver and all passengers, front and rear
explanation: Virginia law requires the driver and all passengers, in both front and rear seats, to wear safety belts.

prompt: A driver transporting anyone younger than age 18 must:
choices: Ensure each is secured in a belt, booster, or child seat, wherever seated
Let them choose whether to buckle
explanation: A driver carrying anyone under 18 must make sure each is properly secured in a safety belt, booster, or child safety seat, no matter where they sit.

prompt: In Virginia, children under what age must be secured in a child safety seat or booster seat?
explanation: All children under age 8 must be properly secured in an approved child safety seat or booster seat.

prompt: A rear-facing child safety seat should never be placed:
choices: In front of an active passenger-side air bag
explanation: Never put a rear-facing child seat in front of an active passenger air bag; the deploying bag can seriously injure the child.

prompt: Children should ride rear-facing:
choices: From birth to at least age 2, or as long as the seat allows
explanation: Children should ride rear-facing from birth to age 2, or as long as the safety-seat manufacturer allows.

prompt: The safest place to install a child safety seat is:
choices: The center of the back seat
explanation: The center of the back seat is the safest position for a properly installed child safety seat.

prompt: Air bags work best when:
choices: The seat is pushed all the way forward
You sit very close to the wheel
explanation: Air bags are designed to work with safety belts, not replace them. Always wear your belt and sit back from the wheel.

prompt: Transporting a child under age 16 in the open bed of a pickup truck in Virginia is:
choices: Legal if they wear a belt
explanation: It is illegal to transport children under age 16 in the bed of a pickup truck, even with a camper shell.

prompt: A Virginia learner's permit allows you to drive only when:
choices: A licensed driver at least 21 (or qualifying relative 18-20) is in the front passenger seat
explanation: With a learner's permit you may drive only when a licensed driver age 21+ — or a qualifying relative who is 18-20 — is seated in the front passenger seat.

prompt: Whenever you drive, Virginia law requires you to:
choices: Carry a valid driver's license or learner's permit
explanation: You must carry a valid driver's license or learner's permit whenever you drive; misuse of either is illegal.

prompt: DMV assigns demerit points to convictions of:
choices: 3, 4, or 6 points depending on the offense
explanation: Convictions are assigned three, four, or six demerit points depending on the seriousness of the violation.

prompt: For a driver under 18, a first conviction of a demerit-point violation results in:
explanation: A first demerit-point (or safety belt/child restraint) conviction committed under age 18 requires completion of a driver improvement clinic.

prompt: If your driver's license is suspended, it means your driving privilege is:
choices: Withdrawn temporarily and can be reinstated
explanation: A suspension withdraws your driving privilege temporarily; you may reinstate it after the suspension period by meeting requirements and paying fees.

prompt: If you fail the knowledge exam and are under age 18, you must wait before retaking it:
explanation: By law, applicants under 18 who fail the knowledge exam must wait a full 15 days before retaking it.

prompt: An adult driver who accumulates 12 demerit points in 12 months will be required to:
explanation: Drivers 18 or older who accumulate 12 demerit points in 12 months (or 18 in 24 months) must complete a driver improvement clinic.
